segment-text: Walk Hard: The Dewey Cox Story Judd Apatow & Jake Kasdan

OVER BLACK, we HEAR the sound of a huge crowd applauding. As it BUILDS into a frenzy of cheering and stomping-CARD - "VERY IMPORTANT FILMS PRESENTS" A SECOND CARD APPEARS-- "WALK HARD-- THE DEWEY COX STORY"
INT. BACKSTAGE AREA - NIGHT

We reveal DEWEY cox, a tall hulking man in his late sixties. He is a combination of Johnny Cash, George Jones, and Waylon Jennings-- an aging badass who has seen it all. Guitar over his shoulder, he leans against a wall in a tragic pose, as though reliving the pain from some awful memory. THE STAGE MANAGER approaches-STAGE MANAGER Mr. Cox *** ? SAM, Dewey's life-long drummer, stops the Stage Manager-SAM Give him a minute, son. Dewey Cox needs to think about his entire life before he plays * TIGHT ON DEWEY-- thinking, remembering, reliving *..
EXT. FARM COUNTRY - MID-1940'S - DUSK
Corn fields swaying in the gentle wind and the golden sun. CARD-- "Springberry, Alabama-- 1944"
INT. FARM HOUSE - DAY
Nine year-old NATE sits at a piano, playing a concerto. The kid is amazing. In the corner, six year-old DEWEY watches, waiting patiently. Nate gets to the end of the piece.
Come on, Nate! Let's go play!

I can't Dewey. I have to practice * PA COX appears in the doorway-- a very strict farmer type (played by an actor in his late-sixties, who is right now wearing a wig to portray the 30 year-old version of himself)-PA COX
Alright. Come on, Dewey, let's go play.
Today is gonna be the best day ever!
EXT. COUNTRY ROAD - DAY
Nate and Dewey both hold sticks as they walk and skip down the road.
What do you want to be when you grow up, Dewey?
I don't know. Never really thunk about it before.
When I grow up, I'm gonna be a concert pee-anist and a
then I'm gonna be an astronaut and I am gonna go to the moon.
Ain't nobody ever gonna step on no
I will. And afterwards, I am gonna be the President of these United States. There's nothing I won't do in this long, long life of mine.
Maybe I'll learn me some pee-ana, too. Then we can be pee-ana playin' brothers .
That's what's great about being young. There's so much time to do great things.
We gonna be alive for sixty or seventy years, you and me!
Enough of this yappin'l Let's go jump across the river!
They run off.
EXT. STREAM - DAY
Nate and Dewey stand at the shore of a fast moving, scarylooking stream.
I bet I can grab that there vine and fly all the way over this here
stream.
I dare you.
Alright then, since you dared me.
Nate takes a long run, GRABS the vine, and FLIES over the
Ha-ha! I did it.
My turn! My turn!
INT. SHED - DAY
Nate and Dewey stand in front of a radial arm saw. holds a piece of wood.
Hey, check out how good I am cutting up this wood.
Make me something real nice.

Nate takes the wood and puts it on the machine and starts cutting it. His shirt gets closer and closer to the blade-it seems he's about to get sucked into this machine. He cuts and shapes furiously, then holds up what he has made. It is a beautiful goose.

That is one fine goose! I'm gonna hang it in my room. QUICK SHOTS OF THEM PLAYING-*Nate runs from a bull and leaps over the fence at the last minute. *Dewey drives a huge tractor, as fast as it will go, barely in control--- chasing Nate, who is galloping on a horse, allout. Nate shows off-- "No hands!" *Nate catches a rattlesnake by hand and tosses it at Dewey, who runs off-DEWEY Nate, you' one crazy fourth grader.
Let's go play "Machete Fight"!
EXT. SHED - DAY
Nate emerges from a shed, carrying two sheathed machetes. He tosses one to Dewey-NATE
Ssshh! ! ! You know how mad Daddy
gets when we play with his
On guard!
Dewey can't resist-- they start to play sword fight with the enormous leather wrapped machetes, very theatrical. Nate backs Dewey to the ground-NATE Do you yeild, sir?
Never!

He "fights back", bumping machetes. Dewey backs Nate down-DEWEY Prepare to meet your maker! He takes the machete back for the "death blow" and as he does, he fails to notice the sheath slide off entirely. He swings at Nate-- AND CUTS HIM IN HALF. Nate's torso SLIDES directly off his legs-- a ridiculous ef feet-- and he stares up at Dewey as they both reali_ze what's just happened-DEWEY Nate!
Dewey! I'm *** I'm *.* I'm halved! Dewey realizes--
We should'a listened to Pa *
Dewey, in case I don't make it, then *** you're just gonna have to be doubly great, for the both of us.
That's a lot of pressure, Nate.
You can handle it, Dewey. Now, run! Get Pa!
INT. COX HOUSE - NIGHT
The Cox family-- MA COX, PA COX, and the seven Cox SIBLINGS-all pray together in the living room. The DOCTOR emerges from a different room, very somber-DOCTOR He's gone. They all SHRIEK in anguish--
I couldn't reattach the top half of his body to the bottom half of his body. Medicine just ain't gotten modern enough for that yet. I'm sorry, folks.
Everyone WAILS. PA COX yells at Dewey-PA COX This is all your fault, Dewey Cox! Ma comes to his defense-MA COX
He cut him in half with a machete!
You sayin' I'm less than a quarter of the boy Nate was? That's what I'm sayin!
PA pulls the table cloth off the table and drapes it over Nate's piano-PA COX
Pa storms out, still raging--
Ma approaches the devastated Dewey, puts an arm around him-MA COX Dewey Cox, run down to the country store. You pick us up some butter.
INT. COUNTRY STORE - DAY

Dewey enters the store and starts looking for the items his mom requested when he hears the sound of a guitar. He walks around a corner and sees a very, very OLD AFRICAN-AMERICAN MAN playing blues guitar. (This old man should actually be played by a one hundred year old actor.) Dewey is hypnotized by the music. Slowly, he approaches the
I ain't never heard no music like that, before. It's so .*. sad.
That's why it's called "the blues", boy.
I think I'd like to play me some blues.
The old man chuckles-OLD MAN Ain't no six year old boy understands the true meaning of the blues.
(earnest) I reckon I might.
You play the guitar?
I never have before. But I'm a real fast learner *
Well, go ahead *..

- He offers Dewey his guitar-- which is way too big for a sixyear old. The old man places Dewey's fingers on the neck-OLD MAN Now hit the strings with your other hand. That's a G. Dewey hits the chord-- it sounds bad. He hits it again-- this time it's perfect.
There you go.

Dewey starts strumming. Then ridiculously quickly he starts playing a perfect 12-bar blues-DEWEY Like this? The old man is spooked-OLD MAN Yeah. Like that ... Then young Dewey starts to sing-- except he has the voice of a 70 year old black man (actually some old guy's voice).
"I done a bad thing/ Done gone and cut my brother in half *..
The old man shakes his head, feeling it, adding "mm-hrns" periodically. Dewey gets lost in it-DEWEY "Yeah, I done a bad, bad thing/
Dewey opens his eyes and spots--- Nate, standing on the other side of the room--- a vision. They lock eyes and nod at each other earnestly.
INT. HIGH SCHOOL GYM - NIGHT

The school talent show, in the gym/auditorium. Folding chairs face a stage at the end of the basketball court. Dewey's parents find seats. I don't even know what we're doing here, at this high school talent show.
We're here to see our boy, that's what we're doing here. Now you sit yourself down. I'll be right
INT. BACK STAGE - SAME
Ma wanders among the TEENAGERS preparing-- a GIRL practices her juggling, a BOY practices his violin-- then she spots-MA COX
Dewey turn to her-- already being played by the actor who
will play him as an adult, except he's wearing a wig. A big acoustic/electric guitar hangs from his shoulder.
Ma, you made it!
There's my favorite 14 year old son!
She has to raise up on her tip toes to kiss his cheek, which embarrasses him-DEWEY
Howdy, boys.
Dewey's BAND (who are actual teenagers) snicker, reply--
Hi./Howdy, Mrs. Cox.
I just wanted to say break a leg. I know you boys are gonna play a real good song.
Thanks, Ma. I'm so lucky to have at least one parent who loves me and supports my dream.
You just go out there and sing your heart out now, ya' hear?
Yes, mama.
She walks away, Dewey turns back to the band-DEWEY Alright, let's just go over that bridge section one more time ***
INT. HIGH SCHOOL GYM - MOMENTS LATER

Ma returns to her seat, where Pa is inexplicably fuming angry. As she makes her way down the row to their seats, she suddenly gets a little dizzy, seems she might pass out-PA COX Ma! He grabs her and helps her to her seat. Those around her are concerned. She touches her head-MAN You alright, ma'am?
I'm fine *.. It's just my vertigo
acting up again.
"Vertigo" *** ?
It's an inner-ear disorder that causes her to lose her balance.
It's nothin' serious. Don't pay me no bother. (MORE)
It's just a little dizziness. Ain't nothin' terrible ever came of no dizziness.
The lights go down. Ma claps, Pa fumes-The MC-- a very square '50s school teacher-- takes the stage-MC Welcome to the annual talent show,
"Talent", my ass. Nate was the only
Now, you hush.
The wrong kid died, goddammit ...
Sshhhh! Now, let's get the evening started with the juggling talents of Amber
Amber juggles her way out on to the stage ...
INT. BACK STAGE - LATER

The show is in progress. Dewey and his band watch from the wings as a very mediocre TAP DANCER finishes her routine. The crowd goes crazy. Dewey is intimidated, turns to his DRUMMER-DEWEY How are we supposed to follow that?
ON STAGE-MC
Let's have a big hand for Delilah
The audience WHOOPS it up again.
And now, sophomore Dewey Cox is going to sing us a song. Ladies and germs *** (laughs at own joke) .** please welcome, Dewey Cox and
the Dewey Cox Four!

The band takes the stage. Dewey stands at the mic. He looks out at the crowd, who wait in judgemental silence. He scans the faces-- half stern-looking ADULTS, half high school KIDS. It's a scary moment for him. He summons his courage-DEWEY I'm Dewey Cox. Here's a little song I wrote. It's called "Take My Hand". And they start the song. It's a very sweet, simple 4-4 early rock song-- in the vein of "That'll be the Day" or "Dream, Dream, Dream". About holding hands with a girl for the first time and walking in the park and looking at the moon ... A little love song with a very gentle beat-- really, really innocuous. At first, the audience stares at them-- a bunch of "what the hell is this?" cut-aways. They've never heard anything like this before. Shocked by the rocking. In the front row, a girl catches Dewey's eye and they exchange smitten looks-- this is EDITH, and we immediately get that she is going to be significant to Dewey. Then-- the KIDS start to.stand up and DANCE. Simultaneously, the adults start to BOO-- disgusted, offended. The kids' dancing gets more intense-- they love this rock music and they are instantly doing The Twist. Dewey bops away on stage, Buddy Holly-esque, dancing with only his bobbing head as he plays his guitar-DEWEY

The adults start to THROW THINGS at the stage. Pa stands and leaves-- Ma tries to stop him, but then lets him go, proud of her boy. A couple of KIDS segue into a really raunchy bump-and-grind, Josephine Baker kind of dance, basically dry-humping each other.
"We'll laugh at all the funny things you say/ Take my hand, let me lead the way *** "

A PREACHER stands up-PREACHER It's the Devil's music!!! A KID standing near him turns and PUNCHES him in the face. Now all of the kids are DANCING and MAKING OUT. And the adults are SCREAMING. A parent THROWS UP. FIGHTS are breaking out, all over ... And as Dewey wraps up the song, the scene devolves into a full-fledged RIOT ...
EXT. FARM HOUSE - NIGHT

A CROWD has gathered on the front lawn of the Cox household. On one side, a group of angry PARENTS are gathered, holding signs, screaming, carrying TORCHES. The Preacher holds an ice pack to his face where he was punched-PREACHER

On the other side of the lawn, separated by COPS, are a bunch of TEENAGERS, looking like "greasers". In one song, Dewey has transformed these sweet preppy kids into rebels without causes. They CHANT-TEENAGERS Dew-ey! Dew-ey! Dew-ey! In a window, Dewey appears from behind a curtain, waves at the kids, who all SCREAM when they see him-INT. FARM HOUSE - SAME --Dewey turns back to his assembled family, satisfied. Dewey's 10 year-old SISTER chimes in-SISTER That there Rock n' Roll music brings out some strong feelings in folks.
I'm just playin' the music I hear in my head, is all.
He moves to Edith-- the girl from the front row-- and puts his arm around her. Pa enters--
That's it! I want you out of this house, boy'!
This boy done gone raised his'self a ruckus! You heard the Preacher-I ain't gonna have no Devil's spawn
Pa, you watch your mouth, now, ya here?! 'Fore you go and say somethin' you gon' regret for the rest of your days ***
It's okay, Mama! Settle down *** The room gets quiet.
Pa's right. Springberry ain't big enough for me, no more. I reckon
it's time for Dewey Cox to move , along .
But where's Dewey Cox gon' go?!
It's time for Dewey Cox to head for the Big City.
But you're only 14! You ain't ready for the pressures of no Big city.
I may be just a tender 14 ... but I got big dreams. An' I reckon destiny's a'callin'.
(tears up) Where's it a'callin' you to, son?
To the Big City. I thought I said that already.
Edith starts to cry-EDITH
Come with me, Edith.
Dewey, I... I ***
I know we just met a half an hour ago. But I love you more than I've ever loved anyone. Come with me to the Big City. What do you say?
I say, yes!
They hug-EDITH
Ma approaches, looks him dead in the eyes--
Don't matter what these people say. You got a gift from the Lord, boy. And don't you never forget it.
Off his stoic close-up, DISSOLVE TO-INT. NIGHTCLUB - NIGHT

An entirely black jazz/blues club, filled with PEOPLE dancing in an outrageously erotic way. It is way too choreographed and the people on the dance floor dance in a .fashion that is so hot that it is hard to watch (in that slightly racist way that these movies so often depict African American.
nightlife).

Dewey, in dirty kitchen whites, holds a mop, as he watches from the kitchen---the HOUSE BAND playing on stage. Bobby Shad and the Bad Men. BOBBY SHAD, the lead singer and lead guitarist is quite cocky, but doesn't quite have the talent to back it up. The band finishes their number-BOBBY SHAD
The band exits the stage, walking past Dewey, who trails
Wow, that was really great! Mr. Shad, I'd love to one day get a chance to play with you and your band. I play a little guitar myself, so if you ever need an extra guitar player, maybe I could ***
Bobby Shad pauses in the doorway of the dressing room-BOBBY SHAD
Yes, Mr. Shad. Anything *
The toilet backstage is overflowing. Can you get that thing cleaned up?
He closes the door in Dewey's face; Dewey is frustrated. The MANAGER has overheard this-MANAGER Dewey, you're here to wash the dishes, not to bother the band.
Dewey heads off, dejected.
INT. RATTY APARTMENT - DAY
Dewey is practicing his guitar in the living room. From the kitchen--
Will you cut out that racket! I am trying to feed your child!
INT. KITCHEN - SAME

Edith (who now looks like a square 1950s housewife) is feeding their BABY. The baby CRIES and has a ridiculous amount of food on her face and clothes. Dewey enters, wearing his guitar over his shoulder and carrying his mop from work.
How am I gonna make it as a famous musician if I don't practice?
Dewey Cox, you gotta get your head out of the clouds.
But it's my dream! I love music. It's all I hear in my head, day and night. Music that could change the world! I just want to play my music for folks. If only I could get the chance **.
She stands up, very melodramatic--

Stop talking about music! (settles down) Now, Daddy said, if we go home, he'd give you a job at the slaughter house. You could make an honest living for yourself and your family. Wouldn't have to walk around in shame, carrying no mop.
Hanging your head, like some kind
I think I'm doing okay for a 15 year old with a wife and a baby!
She moves to him,. touching his face, pleading-EDITH
I want more than that.
She pulls away-EDITH You' saying that you're better than
No, but I can't give up! Music is
my only shot at making a better

The music don't pay for nothing! It's dangerous, this dream of yours. You're playing with our lives, even thinking about "music"! Every time you pick up that guitar to practice, you're taking your daughter's life in your hands! Dewey is hurt.
Give up your dream, Dewey. Dreams don't come true.

I am gonna make this dream come true! Nobody ever said it was gonna be easy! It's hard. It's always hard! It's not easy to walk to the top of a mountain. It's a long, hard walk! Getting to the top is a hard walk, but I plan on walking *** Hard. Right to the top. I will walk hard.
He hears himself say this *.. SUPER-PUSH on Dewey, as he has the greatest idea for a song that he has ever had-DEWEY Walk. Hard.
INT. APARTMENT - BASEMENT - LATER
Dewey is strumming a guitar, pen and pencil in front of him-DEWEY Walk Hard-- when they say it can't
INT. NIGHTCLUB - NIGHT
Dewey is mopping the floor by the kitchen. There is a commotion by the stage in the empty club, Dewey gravitates
I'm gonna have a full house tonight and if Bobby Shad can't play then I need to find someone who will.
(very hoarse) I'm fine. I'll play through the pain.
He holds up his hands, which are bruised and enormous.

No, you won't. Maybe you should have thought about this before you punched your landlord, while you had the laryngitis. The DRUMMER speaks up-DRUMMER Well, I don't know of any band leaders who can be here in the next fifteen minutes.
Maybe there's just no music tonight, Boss.
A tense moment. Then--
'Scuse me *** They all turn to Dewey-DEWEY I play a little. They all consider this-- there doesn't seem to be any other
INT. CLUB - LATER
The club is packed.
BACKSTAGE--

Dewey practices scales. The band watches, concerned, waiting to take the stage. The manager walks over-MANAGER I don't mean to put more pressure on the boy, but "the suits" from the record company are here. ANGLE ON-- A table of HASIDIC JEWS . Have a good show, boys. The band looks nervous, then takes the stage. Dewey stands in front of the microphone and stwnbles, adjusting it for way too long-DEWEY
Get off the stage, white boy!
You ain't ready for the big time.
Let's find out. They start into one of the band's songs-- Dewey is amazing. He sings like Slim Whitman and plays the guitar like Jango
The Hasidic Jews watch, exchange looks-- this kid has
INT. CLUB - AFTER THE SHOW
The band is talking to the Hasidic Jews, who focus on Dewey. L'CHAI'M seems to be in charge-L'CHAI'M
No, sir, Mr. L'chai'm *.*
Bobby Shad runs up in a huff-BOBBY SHAD
saying *.*
Oh, Mr. L'Chai'm .*. all I want is to make a record, like Elvis. Just gimme a chance to show you what I got *** in a big ol' professional studio.
Come see us. We'll see what you can do.
INT. RECORDING STUDIO - DAY A Sun Records-type of recording studio. The PRODUCER (a Sam

Phillips type) and the Hasidic Jews watch from the booth as---Dewey and the Bad Men (who are now his band) play "Moon River" or something like it-- but it's really slow and pretty lame. The Hasidic Jews are disappointed.
Stop, stop, stop. The band stops playing.
Thank you. I've think we've heard
But *** you didn't even let us get
to the bridge ***
I'm sorry, kid, but it's not wowing me. And we need to be wowed, if we're gonna make a record. We need to be hearing somethin' we never heard before. And this *** we've heard it. (MORE)
And worse than that-- it's not really you. You're singing the words, but I'm not believing them. Where's the real Dewey Cox?!
Dewey is stung, quietly-DEWEY You want to hear the real Dewey
I'm not sure there is a real Dewey Cox to hear.
Oh, there's a Dewey Cox, sir. There's a Dewey Cox.
You got any songs of your own?
I got a couple.
The producer looks to the Jews-~
The producer is skeptical-PRODUCER Okay *** You can play one more song.
(solemn) I'll give it a shot.
Dewey starts to strum. The BASS PLAYER whispers-BASS PLAYER Dewey, we don't know this song ***
Just follow me *..

And he starts singing-- "Walk Hard". It is ridiculously emotional. He croons and wails. The band picks it up immediately-- complete with backing "doowop" vocal harmonies. Everyone exchanges looks, struck by the raw power of Dewey's performance. Now he has tears streaming down his face. He could not mean it more. His band is crying. And the Jews are crying. The producer listens, concentrating *** then suddenly, starts QUIVERING, spastically-- having a religious experience. The Jews nod to each other, then to the producer, who nods back, still quivering *** BEGIN MONTAGE-- to the finished song.
INT. DJ'S BOOTH - DAY
INSERT-- a record placed on a turntable * A very enthusiastic DJ, into his mic-DJ Hot off the presses, here's a brand new song from a youngster named Dewey Cox!
INT. DINER - DAY
As the song plays over the radio, a KID notices-KID Hey, turn it up, pops! The COUNTER MAN turns up the radio. Immediately, all of the TEENAGERS stand and start dancing in the diner.
EXT. DEWEY'S APARTMENT BUILDING - DAY
Dewey steps outside, mop in hand, on his way *to work and is instantly SWARMED by TEENAGERS--
What the ...
They're playing your song for the first time!
INT. DJ'S BOOTH - DAY
A DIFFERENT DJ spins the record-DJ 2 That's "Walk Hard" by Dewey Cox, soaring up the charts to number INSERT-"The Chart"-- as "Walk Hard" climbs from 7 to 3 ...
EXT. PARKING LOT - DAY
The PRESS snap photos as the Jews hand Dewey the keys to his brand new Mustang. Dewey takes the keys and GRINS at the cameras-- then is immediately SWARMED again. The chart-- "Walk Hard" reaches #1!
INT. DEWEY'S NEW HOUSE - DAY
The Jews show Dewey and a very pregnant Edith around their new enormous ranch house.
INT. FARM HOUSE - NIGHT
Back home, Dewey's parents listen to their radio. Ma is ecstatically excited, Pa is angry.*
That's my boy!
Ma gets up and starts DANCING along-PA COX You be careful, now *** with your vertigo.
Hush now, Pa! Ain't nothin' wrong
with a little boogie *.* As she says this, she looses her balance and FALLS OVER.
INT. DELIVERY ROOM - DAY

Edith is GIVING BIRTH. A DOCTOR pulls a newborn BABY from Edith's body, hands it to Dewey. Then-- the Doctor pulls another BABY out of Edith, and hands this one to DEWEY also-DEWEY Twins?! Thank you, Lord! The Doctor pulls a third CHILD from-DEWEY Triplets! Dewey tries to hold all three new babies, which is not easy. Then a bunch of NURSES run in-NURSE Dewey Cox?! --and SWARM him.
INT. DEWEY'S NEW HOUSE - NIGHT
Edith and the several babies sit on the couch, covering their eyes. Dewey enters, carrying-- a Chimpanzee~
Okay!
Edith sees and gets really excited-EDITH A monkey!
INT. KITCHEN - NIGHT

At the dinner table-- Dewey, Edith, four babies and the chimp are all laughing together, having a great time. Life is good. Then-- a giraffe sticks it's head through the open window. Dewey feeds it a grape off the diner table. END MONTAGE.
INT. DEWEY'S BEDROOM - DAY
As Dewey packs his bag, Edith holds the triplets, upset--
*** it'll just be for a few weeks *
Who's gonna help me take care of these children?
Your mama said she'd help out and we can get baby sitters, if we need to.
You gonna let some baby sitter be a father to your four infants?
This is my job now, baby! I'm playing music for people who want to hear me! This is all I've ever wanted!
She looses it--
What about your family?! Look, what our life has become! I never see you, the kids never see you **.
I've only been popular for two months!
You missed their Christening!
I had a gig! I made us three hundred dollars that night. How else we gon' pay for all this?! Aren't you happy with your new house and your fancy clothes and your monkey and your giraffe?!
They took the giraffe away! Said it was illegal to have in a private home or some such nonsense ***
Then I'll get you a llama! Or *** there's this certain kind of halfdog/ half-wolf that people say makes a good exotic pet *..
This ain't about no exotic pets!
She runs to the bathroom and SLAMS the door. The babies CRY. DEWEY
He POUNDS on the door-DEWEY Come on, baby! Come out of the bathroom ***
INT. BATHROOM - SAME
Edith leans against the door, crying-EDITH Mama says there's a lot of evil out there, on the road. Temptations. Make a man do evil things.
(laughs) Edith! That's ridiculous! There ain't no evil, out there. I'm just gon' be singing my songs for folks. That's all.
She considers this, but doesn't come out.

segment-speaker: DEWEY Edith, baby *** you know how much I
segment-text: love you. I would never do nothin' that might hurt this perfect life we got. You and this here fastgrowing family of ours is the only thing that matters to me. I mean, a life without you ... would be no life at all.
Another light bulb moment-DEWEY
"Life without you ...
INT. THEATER - NIGHT
A lively CROWD twists along to-- Chuck Berry, who's on stage, playing "Johnny B Good".
INT. BACK STAGE - SAME
Dewey and the band watch from the wings, awed by Chuck's showmanship and guitar playing.
How we supposed to follow that? MAN (OS)
Don't worry, Dewey *** He turns to-- BUDDY HOLLY (Frankie Munitz cameo).
*** you'll do great.
Thanks, Buddy Holly.
And if you don't, me and the Crickets'll pick it right back up. You got nothing to worry about.
The STAGE MANAGER approaches-STAGE MANAGER
Okay, boys-- change of plans. Elvis
wants to get out of here early tonight. He's hungry. And his
favorite steak house closes at
nine. So here's the new order-when Chuck finishes, Buddy'll go on next, then Elvis-- then you, Dewey.
(playing it cool) Okay ... So it's Buddy Holly, then Elvis ... then me.
Just for tonight.
Okay. I'm just happy to be here .
EXT. ALLEY - NIGHT
Dewey is CRYING in the alley, terrified-DEWEY I ain't good enough to follow
CHILD'S VOICE (O.S.) What you talking about, Dewey? Dewey turns to the voice--.,....it's "a vision" of his brother Nate. DEWEY I ain't ready, Nate.
I thought you was gonna be double great, for the both of us.
I'm trying, Nate. But hell, ain't nobody can follow Elvis ***
You can.
You really think so?
I know so. This is our dream,
Dewey. I guess dreams come do true. Dewey stands up straighter, gathering the strength-DEWEY Yes, they do.
You go out there and play-- and I'll be there on stage with you.
Dewey nods, growing determined-DEWEY
Just ..* this freaks me out, a little.
Okay. Point taken. Dewey!
Dewey turns to him-STAGE MANAGER The big guy's on his final encore.
Dewey stares ahead, stoic ***
INT. BACK STAGE - MOMENTS LATER
Dewey heads for the stage, passes a sweaty ELVIS (another teen star cameo)--
I got 'em good and riled for you, Dewey.
Thanks, Elvis Presley.
(to his band) Come on, boys, let's go find us some ladies and get us some meat! Dewey watches them walk off-DEWEY I wonder how Mrs. Presley would feel about that.
INT. STAGE - SAME
Dewey and his band (complete with three pretty BACK UP SINGERS) assume the stage. Dewey steps to the mic, stares out at the crowd who once again stare back, very judgemental--
Good evening. I'm Dewey cox. Looks like I got myself one skeptical
They stare back at him.
This is a song I wrote for a very

He starts into "A Life Without You Is No Life At All"-- an enormous, soaring Roy Orbison-esque love ballad. A heart-onsleeve confessional about his deep love for his wife.
